coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots modified anatase/rutile TiO2 photoanode with dramatically enhanced photoelectrochemical performance
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Tingsheng
Chen, Shuai
Li, Linsen
Wang, Jiachen
Zhang, Yan
Li, Jinhua
Bai, Jing
Xia, Ligang
Xu, Qunjie
Rahim, Mohammadi
Zhou, Baoxue
coredata.dc:description: TiO2 is a promising photoanode material for photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting, but its severe bulk recombination of photogenerated carriers, sluggish oxygen evolution reaction (OER) kinetics and poor visible light response are the main bottleneck problems. Here, the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modified anatase/rutile TiO2 photoanode (CQDs/A/R-TiO2) was designed by growth of anatase TiO2 nanothorns on rutile TiO2 nanorods and further surface modification of CQDs. The results revealed that the A/R-TiO2 heterojunction significantly suppressed the bulk recombination of photogenerated carriers. With further incorporation of CQDs into A/R-TiO2, dramatical improvement of OER kinetics and ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) light harvesting were obtained. The bulk charge separation efficiency (η bulk) and surface charge injection efficiency (η surface) of CQDs/A/R-TiO2 are 1.69 and 5.74 times higher than that of the pristine TiO2 at 0.6 V vs. RHE, respectively. Meanwhile, the photocurrent is increased by 11.72 times and the onset potential is negatively shifted by 240 mV for CQDs/A/R-TiO2.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Anatase/rutile TiO2 heterojunction
Carbon quantum dots
PEC
UV–vis light harvesting
Bulk and surface charge transfer kinetics

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and structural characterization of the photoanodes
Carriers separation and transfer kinetics processes of the phoanodes
Bulk separation and surface injection efficiencies
Energy band alignment and carriers transfer mechanism

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image and size distribution of CQDs, (b) HRTEM image of CQDs, (c) Raman spectrum of CQDs and (d) PL emission spectra of CQDs with different excitation wavelengths (inset: the photograph of CQDs solution).
(a) Top-view and (b) cross-sectional SEM images of TiO2, (c) top-view and (d) cross-sectional SEM images of A/R-TiO2, (e) top-view SEM image and (f) element mapping of CQDs/A/R-TiO2.
(a) XRD patterns, (b) high-resolution Ti 2p spectra and (c) high-resolution O 1s spectra of TiO2, A/R-TiO2, CQDs/TiO2 and CQDs/A/R-TiO2. (d) High-resolution C 1s spectrum of CQDs/A/R-TiO2.

PEC performances of TiO2, A/R-TiO2, CQDs/TiO2 and CQDs/A/R-TiO2 photoanodes. (a) LSV curves, (b) comparison of the onset potentials and photocurrent densities at different potentials obtained from Fig. 4a, (c) transient photocurrent densities measured at 1.0 V vs RHE, (d) ABPE, (e) IPCE at 1.23 V vs. RHE. (f) Long-term stabilities recorded at 1.23 V vs. RHE under illumination of 100 mW cm−2 for TiO2 and CQDs/A/R-TiO2 photoanodes.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectra and (b) Tauc plots of TiO2, A/R-TiO2, CQDs/TiO2 and CQDs/A/R-TiO2.

(a) The calculated current density fluxes and the integrated current densities (J abs), (b) LSV plots measured in the electrolytes of 0.2 M Na2SO4 + 1 M Na2SO3 under illumination of 100 mW cm−2 (the dotted lines were measured in dark), (c) bulk charge separation efficiencies (η bulk) and (d) surface charge injection efficiencies (η surface) of TiO2, A/R-TiO2, CQDs/TiO2 and CQDs/A/R-TiO2.
EIS Nyquist plots of TiO2, A/R-TiO2, CQDs/TiO2 and CQDs/A/R-TiO2 under illumination of 100 mW cm−2. The inset is the equivalent circuit model.

(a) IMPS and (b) IMVS Nyquist plots, (c) electron transport time (τ c) and electron recombination lifetime (τ r), (d) electron collection efficiencies (η c) and electron average diffusion lengths (L n) of TiO2, A/R-TiO2, CQDs/TiO2 and CQDs/A/R-TiO2.
(a) TPC and (b) TPV curves of TiO2, A/R-TiO2, CQDs/TiO2 and CQDs/A/R-TiO2. The curves are fitted to a bi-exponential decay model, y = y0 + A1e−x/τ1 +A2e-x/τ2.

(a) VB XPS spectra and (b) Mott-Schottky plots of rutile TiO2 and anatase TiO2, (c) CV curve of CQDs, (d) schematic diagram of the proposed charge transfer mechanism of ternary CQDs/A/R-TiO2 heterojunction.
Schematic illustration of the preparation of CQDs/A/R-TiO2.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots modified anatase/rutile TiO2 photoanode with dramatically enhanced photoelectrochemical performance

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: TiO2 is a promising photoanode material for photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting, but its severe bulk recombination of photogenerated carriers, sluggish oxygen evolution reaction (OER) kinetics and poor visible light response are the main bottleneck problems. Here, the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modified anatase/rutile TiO2 photoanode (CQDs/A/R-TiO2) was designed by growth of anatase TiO2 nanothorns on rutile TiO2 nanorods and further surface modification of CQDs. The results revealed that the A/R-TiO2 heterojunction significantly suppressed the bulk recombination of photogenerated carriers. With further incorporation of CQDs into A/R-TiO2, dramatical improvement of OER kinetics and ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) light harvesting were obtained. The bulk charge separation efficiency (η bulk) and surface charge injection efficiency (η surface) of CQDs/A/R-TiO2 are 1.69 and 5.74 times higher than that of the pristine TiO2 at 0.6 V vs. RHE, respectively. Meanwhile, the photocurrent is increased by 11.72 times and the onset potential is negatively shifted by 240 mV for CQDs/A/R-TiO2.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Bulk and surface charge transfer kinetics

ce:section.ce:para: Owing to the growing severity of energy crisis and environmental emissions, the demands for clean and sustainable energy supplies are of great urgency. Hydrogen is an ideal energy carrier to meet the challenges for the fact that hydrogen with a high energy density is zero-emission and renewable. Hydrogen generation by photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting is the research hotspot due to the capacity of solar energy storage in chemical bonds [1,2].

The efficiency of PEC water splitting mainly depends on the properties of photoanode materials. Researchers have made unremitting efforts to exploit highly active photoanode materials, including ZnO [3], TiO2 [4], WO3 [5], BiVO4 [6], and Fe2O3 [7], etc. Among the metal oxide semiconductors, TiO2is one of the most promising photoanode materials due to its abundant storage, low cost, good stability, nontoxicity and high intrinsic photocatalytic activity [8–13]. Particularly, one-dimensional (1D) TiO2 nanorods were most studied for the large surface area, enhanced light absorption, short diffusion distance for photogenerated carriers [8,11–13]. However, the low electron mobility (1 cm2/V/s), short hole diffusion length (10−100 nm) and large band gaps (∼3.0 eV for rutile TiO2 and ∼3.2 eV for anatase TiO2) lead to severe bulk recombination of photogenerated carriers, sluggish hole transfer kinetics at the photoanode/electrolyte interface and poor visible light response, respectively [8–13]. These shortcomings seriously block the PEC activity of TiO2 photoanode. In order to solve these problems, plenty of strategies have been adopted, such as element doping [14,15], introduction of oxygen vacancy [11,16], quantum dot sensitization [11,17], surface decoration of oxygen evolution catalysts (OECs) [15,18,19] and construction of heterojunction [9,10,12,13], to boost the PEC properties of TiO2 photoanodes.

Construction of TiO2-based heterojunction is one of the most efficient approaches to suppress the bulk recombination of photogenerated carriers. Meanwhile, heterojunction with matched band levels, large contact area and good stability is highly required. It was found that the anatase/rutile TiO2 heterojunctions exhibited better photocatalytic or PEC activities than the pure rutile or anatase TiO2, which is due to the matched band levels for built-in electric fields, resulting in efficient separation of photogenerated carriers [9,10,20]. Besides, the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) as the key half reaction in the photoanode is generally limited by the thermodynamically multiple electron and proton processes [15,18,19]. Therefore, surface decoration of OECs is an important method to boost the surface charge injection efficiency by improving the OER kinetics. For example, P. Wen et al. [19] reported the Ni2P nanocrystals coated TiO2 nanorod-inverse opal photoanode (Ni2P/NR-IO), which showed more than 2-fold enhancement of photocurrent density compared with the pristine NR-IO at 1.23 V vs. RHE.

As an emerging photoelectrical material, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have plenty of advantages due to their unique properties, such as high electron mobility, low cost, good stability, nontoxicity and natural abundance [21]. It has been reported that the CQDs can serve as an ultrafast charge channel for PEC devices and can efficiently improve the OER kinetics of photoanodes [22–24]. For example, X. Lv et al. [23] and P. Luan et al. [24] reported the CQDs/NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 and CQDs/BiVO4 photoanodes, respectively, both finding that the surface charge injection efficiencies were greatly increased after decoration of CQDs. Additionally, the CQDs could contribute to the cathodic shifts of the onset potentials for PEC water oxidation [23–25]. Besides, the CQDs can also improve the ultraviolet light absorbing capacity and expand the range of light harvesting to the visible region of the solar spectrum of photoanodes. Some recent works indicated that the CQDs could increase the visible light absorption of photoanodes, such as CQDs/ZnO [26], CQDs/TiO2 [11], CQDs/BiVO4 [1], CQDs/WO3 [27] and CQDs/Fe2O3 [28], etc.

Based on the above discussion, a novel CQDs/A/R-TiO2 photoanode was designed by growth of anatase TiO2 nanothorns on rutile TiO2 nanorods (A/R-TiO2) and further surface modification of CQDs (Scheme 1 ). Compared with the pristine TiO2 at 0.6 V vs. RHE, the photocurrent is increased by 11.72 times and the onset potential is negatively shifted by 240 mV for CQDs/A/R-TiO2. It mainly benefits from the following aspects: (i) the A/R-TiO2 heterojunction significantly suppresses the bulk recombination of photogenerated carriers; (ii) the CQDs efficiently improve the OER kinetics; (iii) the CQDs sharply boost the light harvesting both in the ultraviolet and visible regions.

The CQDs were characterized by transmission electronic microscope (TEM), Raman and photoluminescence (PL) emission spectroscopy, which are exhibited in Fig. 1 . It can be observed from the TEM image that the CQDs are uniformly distributed in the liquid phase with good dispersion. The size distribution of CQDs ranges from 1 to 3 nm, and the average size of CQDs is 1.76 nm (Fig. 1a). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of CQDs is shown in Fig. 1b. The lattice spacing of 0.234 and 0.236 nm corresponds to the (100) facet of graphite [27]. Two sharp peaks locate at 1354 cm−1 and 1582 cm−1 are observed in Fig. 1c, which are indexed to the D and G bands of carbon materials [29–32]. The CQDs show the PL spectra with different excitation wavelengths (350, 400, 450, 500 550 and 600 nm) (Fig. 1d). The wavelength of the emission peak shifts to higher value as the excitation wavelength increases, which probably originates from the surface defect emission of CQDs [1].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and structural characterization of the photoanodes
ce:section.ce:para: It can be clearly observed from the scanning electron microscope (SEM) images that the TiO2 film is consisted of dense TiO2 nanorods with average width of 150 nm and length of 3.0 μm, which are uniformly and vertically aligned on the FTO conductive substrate (Fig. 2 a and Fig. 2b). In addition, the structure evolution of A/R-TiO2 films prepared with different concentration of ammonium hexafluorotitanate (AH) are compared in Fig. S1. With increased concentration of AH (5, 10, 15 and 20 mM, denoted as A/R-TiO2-5, A/R-TiO2-10, A/R-TiO2-15 and A/R-TiO2-20, respectively), the density and length of anatase TiO2 thorns are increasing. The density and length of anatase TiO2 thorns in the A/R-TiO2-5 are very small. And the anatase TiO2 thorns in the A/R-TiO2-20 seem somewhat dense. The A/R-TiO2-15 is optimal, which shows the highest photocurrent density for water oxidation (Fig. S2). It can be observed from Fig. 2c and Fig. S3 (enlarged SEM image) that the anatase TiO2 thorns in the A/R-TiO2-15 uniformly grow on TiO2 nanorods with moderate density, dispersion, length (less than 50 nm) and thickness (less than 20 nm). Compared with the pristine TiO2 film, the thickness change of A/R-TiO2-15 film is ignorable (Fig. 2d). Fig. S4 and Fig. S5 are the SEM image and linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curve for PEC water oxidation of pure anatase TiO2, respectively. The anatase TiO2 consists of uniform nanoparticles with diameter of about 20 nm, showing very low photocurrent density.

To get the optimal CQDs/TiO2 film, the load of CQDs was adjusted by controlling the spin-coating times of CQDs solution. The CQDs/TiO2 films prepared with 1, 2 and 3 times of spin-coating were denoted as CQDs/TiO2-1, CQDs/TiO2-2 and CQDs/TiO2-3, respectively. Their absorbance and LSV curve for PEC water oxidation are compared in Fig. S6 and Fig. S7, respectively. The CQDs/TiO2 films show dramatically increased ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) light harvesting compared with the pristine TiO2 film, and the light harvesting is greatly boosted with more load of CQDs. The results imply the successful decoration of CQDs on TiO2 films and the remarkable improvement of UV–vis light harvesting by incorporation of CQDs into TiO2 films. All the CQDs/TiO2 films show higher photocurrent densities than the pristine TiO2. And the photocurrent density of CQD/TiO2-3 is lower than that of CQD/TiO2-2, possibly due to the aggregation of CQDs with excessive times of spin-coating. As shown in Fig. 2e, there is no morphology change for CQDs/A/R-TiO2 from the SEM image after decoration of CQDs. The energy dispersive X-ray spectrum (EDS) and element mapping indicate the uniform distribution of C element on the surface of CQDs/A/R-TiO2 with the atomic ratio of 10.36 at% (Fig. 2f, Fig. S8 and Table S1). In addition to CQDs, the C element might be detected due to possibly carbonaceous contamination on the surface of the film. To confirm the origination of C element in CQDs/A/R-TiO2, the EDS and element mapping of the pristine TiO2 were further supplemented, which did not detect any C signal (Fig. S9, Fig. S10 and Table S2), suggesting a negligible C contamination on the surface of the film. The results imply the successful incorporation of CQDs into A/R-TiO2.

The crystal structures and compositions of TiO2, A/R-TiO2, CQDs/TiO2 and CQDs/A/R-TiO2 were further characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The diffraction peaks appearing at 26.59°, 33.87°, 37.72°, 51.83°, 61.72° and 65.89° are well indexed to SnO2 on the FTO substrates (PDF#46-1088) (Fig. 3 a). The diffraction peaks in the pattern of TiO2 are perfectly indexed to the rutile-structured TiO2 (PDF#75-1748). The new appeared diffraction peaks centered at 25.3° for A/R-TiO2 and CQDs/A/R-TiO2 are well indexed to the (101) facet of anatase TiO2 (PDF#73-1764), confirming the successful synthesis of A/R-TiO2 heterojunction. In addition, no obvious diffraction peak of CQDs was detected for CQDs/TiO2 and CQDs/A/R-TiO2, possibly resulting from the small load of CQDs. The XRD pattern of anatase TiO2 film is exhibited in Fig. S11.

The XPS scan survey spectra of TiO2, A/R-TiO2, CQDs/TiO2 and CQDs/A/R-TiO2 are displayed in Fig. S12. The obvious peaks of Ti 2p and O 1s in TiO2 and A/R-TiO2 are characteristics of TiO2, and the relatively weaker peaks of C 1s are ascribed to the carbonaceous contamination [33]. Owing to the additional carbon from CQDs, the CQDs/TiO2 and CQDs/A/R-TiO2 show increased intensities of C 1s peaks. Fig. 3b displays the high-resolution Ti 2p spectra of TiO2, A/R-TiO2, CQDs/TiO2 and CQDs/A/R-TiO2. The peaks centered at the binding energies (BE) of 463.6–464.1 eV and 457.9–458.4 eV successively correspond to Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2, coinciding with Ti4+ values in TiO2 lattice [10,11]. Compared with rutile TiO2, the positive shifts of Ti 2p peaks are observed after the combination with anatase TiO2, providing evidence of an interaction between rutile TiO2 and anatase TiO2. After incorporation of CQDs, the Ti 2p peaks shift to higher BE, which is possibly attributed to the presence of Ti–C interaction in CQDs/TiO2 and CQDs/A/R-TiO2 [34,35]. As shown in Fig. 3c, two oxygen chemical states can be observed for the photoanodes, in which the BE of 529.1–530.0 eV and 531.2–532.0 eV are assigned to lattice oxygen (OL) and hydroxyl oxygen (OH), respectively [9]. The OL exists in the form of bonding to Ti4+ ions in TiO2. Obviously, the O 1s peaks also shift to higher BE once the CQDs are decorated on the pristine TiO2 or A/R-TiO2, which is possibly due to the new binding between O of Ti–O and C [34,35]. The positive shifts of the BE for both Ti 2p and O 1s indicate a decrease in the electron density of TiO2. This can be ascribed to the surface binding interaction between TiO2 and CQDs, which induces an electron transfer from TiO2 to CQDs [35]. In addition, the proportions of Ti − OH in TiO2, A/R-TiO2, CQDs/TiO2 and CQDs/A/R-TiO2 are calculated to be 30.5 %, 36.8 %, 65.5 % and 67.4 %, respectively. Noticeably, the realtive content of TiOH is significantly increased after modification of CQDs, which may be caused by the additional CO or COH groups of CQDs [34]. It has been reported that the hydroxyl species at the surface of catalyst can enhance the OER activity [36]. Therefore, after incorporation of CQDs, the resulting CQDs/TiO2 and CQDs/A/R-TiO2 show significantly enhanced PEC performances. As shown in Fig. 3d, three peaks centered at 284.8, 286.1, and 288.1 eV are assigned to the CC, CO, and OCO groups, respectively, suggesting the existence of CQDs [11,37]. For comparison, the high-resolution Ti 2p spectra and O 1s spectra of anatase TiO2 are displayed in Fig. S13. The BE of Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 are 458.9 eV and 464.6 eV, respectively. The proportion of Ti − OH is calculated to be 34.0 %.

Fig. 4 demonstrates the PEC performances of TiO2, A/R-TiO2, CQDs/TiO2 and CQDs/A/R-TiO2 photoanodes. Compared with the pristine TiO2, the A/R-TiO2 shows greatly increased photocurrent density with a small negatively shifted onset potential, while the CQDs/TiO2presents significantly enhanced photocurrent density with a remarkable negatively shifted onset potential (Fig. 4a). Additionally, the CQDs/A/R-TiO2 further shows much steeper and larger photocurrent density. The onset potentials and photocurrent densities at different potentials for TiO2, A/R-TiO2, CQDs/TiO2 and CQDs/A/R-TiO2 are compared in Fig. 4b and Fig. S14. Specifically, the onset potential of A/R-TiO2 is 0.69 V vs. RHE, which is negatively shifted by 40 mV compared with TiO2 (0.73 V vs. RHE). However, the onset potentials of TiO2 and A/R-TiO2 are negatively shifted by 200 mV after modification of CQDs, suggesting that the CQDs can greatly reduce the onset potential of TiO2. The CQDs/A/R-TiO2 shows the photocurrent densities of 0.88 mA cm−2 at 0.6 V vs. RHE, 1.89 mA cm−2 at 0.9 V vs. RHE and 2.76 mA cm−2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE, which are 11.72, 4.72 and 2.98 times higher than TiO2, 8.77, 2.66 and 1.62 times higher than A/R-TiO2, and 1.71, 1.63 and 1.49 times higher than CQDs/TiO2, respectively. The transient photocurrent responses of the photoanodes were studied by chronoamperometric current method at 1.0 V vs. RHE under chopped illumination (Fig. 4c). The results are in good agreement with the LSV data as shown in Fig. 4a. In addition, a sharp transient spike is observed for the pristine TiO2 upon removing the light barrier, because the photogenerated holes accumulate at the surface and then recombine with the photogenerated electrons [38]. However, the transient spikes are obviously attenuated for A/R-TiO2, CQDs/TiO2 and CQDs/A/R-TiO2, indicating that the formation of A/R-TiO2 heterojunction and surface decoration of CQDs can efficiently suppress the recombination of the holes reaching the surface of TiO2 photoanode.

Assuming 100 % of Faradaic efficiency, the applied bias photon-to-current efficiency (ABPE) can be calculated by the Eq. (1):
(1)
A B P E
%
=
J ( 1.23 - V )
P
× 100
where J is the photocurrent density (mA cm−2), V is the applied bias (V vs. RHE) and P is the incident light power density (100 mW cm−2). As shown in Fig. 4d, the maximum ABPE of TiO2 is only 0.13 % at 0.92 V vs. RHE, while the ABPE of A/R-TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 can reach the maximum of 0.24 % at 0.95 V vs. RHE and 0.40 % at 0.81 V vs. RHE, respectively. In contrast, the maximum ABPE of CQDs/A/R-TiO2 is further increased to 0.68 % at the lowest potential of 0.80 V vs. RHE, which is more than fivefold higher than that of the pristine TiO2. The incident photon-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE) was measured as a function of the incident light wavelength (300∼600 nm) ( Fig. 4e). The IPCE can be calculated by the formula:
(2)
I P C E
%
=
1240 J ( λ )
λ P ( λ )
× 100
where J(λ), λ and P(λ) are the measured photocurrent density at specific wavelength, the wavelength of incident light and the measured light power density at that wavelength, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4e, the as-prepared photoanodes all display the maximum IPCE at the wavelength of 390 nm. The maximum IPCE of TiO2 is only 23.4 %, while the IPCE of A/R-TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 can reach the maximum of 48.7 % and 55.7 %, respectively. The maximum IPCE of CQDs/A/R-TiO2 is further increased to 75.3 %. The photocurrent response of TiO2 is mainly located at the wavelength range from 360 to 410 nm and declines to almost zero below 360 nm or above 410 nm. However, the photocurrent response of A/R-TiO2 is extended to the ultraviolet region from 360 to 300 nm, which is mainly attributed to theanatase TiO2 nanothorns that enhance the ultraviolet light harvesting. In addition, the photocurrent response is increased both in the ultraviolet and visible light regions after modification of CQDs (see the inset), which improves the PEC activities of CQDs/TiO2 and CQD/A/R-TiO2 over a wide wavelength range from 300 to 600 nm. Moreover, a long-term photocurrent versus time (i–t) was measured to observe the PEC stability of CQDs/A/R-TiO2. As shown in Fig. 4f, the photocurrent densities match the LSV results well. The TiO2 shows superior stability without any photocurrent decay after 5 h of continuous irradiation. Under the same conditions, the CQDs/A/R-TiO2 retains more than 92.3 % of initial photocurrent, suggesting its excellent PEC durability.

The light absorption properties of TiO2, A/R-TiO2, CQDs/TiO2 and CQDs/A/R-TiO2 were measured over the wavelength range of 300−600 nm by the UV–vis absorption spectra. After growth of anatase TiO2 thorns on rutile TiO2 nanorods, the resulting A/R-TiO2 displays a small blue shift due to a higher band gap energy (E g) of anatase TiO2, indicating the well-developed A/R-TiO2 heterojunction was successfully fabricated. Compared with the pristine TiO2, a significantly increased absorbance can be observed below the wavelength of 380 nm, which is ascribed to the branched structure of anatase TiO2 thorns that efficiently enhances the ultraviolet light harvesting. As expected, after decoration of CQDs, the resulting CQDs/TiO2 and CQDs/A/R-TiO2 show obvious red shifts and remarkable light absorption over the whole wavelength range (UV–vis light). In addition, the higher absorbance of CQDs/A/R-TiO2 than CQDs/ TiO2 above the wavelength of 400 nm is also attributed to branched structure of anatase TiO2 thorns that boost the visible light harvesting. The E g of the photoanodes are calculated by Kubelka-Munk transformation [1]:
(3)
αhv = A(hv − E g )2
where α, h, v and A are the absorption coefficient, Planck's constant, light frequency and proportionality constant, respectively. From the Tauc plots in Fig. 6b, the E g values of TiO2, A/R-TiO2, CQDs/TiO2, and CQDs/A/R-TiO2 are estimated to be 3.00, 3.03, 2.93 and 2.93 eV, respectively. The absorption edge of anatase TiO2 locates at approximately 385.8 nm, giving the E g of 3.21 eV (Fig. S15).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carriers separation and transfer kinetics processes of the phoanodes
Bulk separation and surface injection efficiencies
ce:section.ce:para:
To reveal the reasons of significantly improved photocurrent for CQDs/A/R-TiO2, the charge separation and transfer behaviors of the photoanodes were investigated. The photocurrent density for water oxidation (J PEC) can be expressed as [39,40].
(4)
J PEC = J abs × η bulk × η surface
where J abs is the photocurrent density calculated by assuming 100 % conversion of the absorbed photons to current density [41]; bulk charge separation efficiency (η bulk) is the yield of the photogenerated holes reaching the surface, and surface charge injection efficiency (η surface) is the yield of the surface holes injected into the electrolyte [42]. The J abs can be estimated from the AM 1.5 G solar spectrum and light harvesting efficiency (LHE) (Fig. S16) according to the following Eqs. [43,44]:
(5)
J
a b s
=
∫ 300
λ e
λ 1240
· N
p h
λ
·
L H E ( λ ) d λ
(6) LHE = 1 – 10−A (
λ
)
where λ is the wavelength (nm); λe is the absorption edge wavelength of the photoanodes; N ph (λ) is the photon flux (mW cm−2 nm−1); A (λ) is the absorbance at wavelength λ. According to the UV–vis absorption spectra in Fig. 5
a, the λe of TiO2, A/R-TiO2, CQDs/TiO2 and CQDs/A/R-TiO2 are 413.4, 409.3, 422.8 and 422.8 nm, giving the J abs of 3.16, 2.98, 4.08 and 4.12 mA cm−2, respectively (Fig. 6a). In the presence of hole scavenger (Na2SO3), the surface recombination of charge carriers can be completely suppressed without influencing the η bulk (η surface = 100 %). Therefore, the η bulk and η surface can be calculated by the Eqs. [13,45]:
(7)
η
b u l k
=
J
N
a 2
S
O 3
J
a b s
(8)
η
s u r f a c e
=
J
H 2
O
J
N a
2
S
O 3
where J H2O and J Na2SO3 are the photocurrent densities achieved by the oxidation of H2O and Na2SO3, respectively (Fig. 4a and Fig. 6b, respectively).

According to the formulas above, the calculated η bulk and η surface are presented in Fig. 6c and Fig. 6d, respectively. The η bulk and η surface of CQDs/A/R-TiO2 are far higher than that of TiO2. At the potentials of 0.6 and 1.23 V vs. RHE, the η bulk of CQDs/A/R-TiO2 are 1.69 and 1.78 times higher than that of TiO2, respectively. At the lower potential of 0.4 V vs. RHE, the η surface of TiO2 is close to zero, while the η surface of CQDs/A/R-TiO2 is up to ∼20 %. At the potentials of 0.6 and 0.8 V vs. RHE, the η surface of CQDs/A/R-TiO2 are 5.74 and 2.98 times higher than that of TiO2, respectively. It can be clearly observed that the formation of A/R-TiO2 heterojunction and surface decoration of CQDs can both contribute to the enhancement of η bulk and η surface, while the former primarily boosts the η bul
k and the latter mianly increases the η surface. In addition, the trends of η surface curves are similar to that of LSV curves in Fig. 4a, suggesting that the increased η surface can effectively reduce the overpotentials of the photoanodes. It is precisely because of the simultaneously improvement of η bulk and η surface that more holes are accessible to the electrode/electrolyte interface for efficient PEC water oxidation, resulting in dramatically enhanced photocurrent of CQDs/A/R-TiO2.

To gain more insight into the charge transfer kinetics at the interface of electrode/electrolyte, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was recorded at 1.23 V vs. RHE under illumination (100 mW cm−2). The EIS Nyquist plots of the photoanodes are exhibited in Fig. 7 , with decreased radius in the order: TiO2 > A/R-TiO2 > CQDs/TiO2 > CQDs/A/R-TiO2. The smallest radius of CQDs/A/R-TiO2 implies its faster charge transfer kinetics at the electrode/electrolyte interface for water oxidation [1,44]. Furthermore, the EIS plots are fitted to an equivalent circuit model shown in the inset of Fig. 7 using Zview software, in which the Rs, Rct and CPE represent the series resistance, charge transfer resistance and constant phase element at the electrode/electrolyte interface, respectively. The CQDs/A/R-TiO2 has the smallest Rct value of 233 Ω, compared with TiO2 (798 Ω), A/R-TiO2 (534 Ω) and CQDs/TiO2 (380 Ω), demonstrating the significantly enhanced charge separation and transfer kinetics due to the formation of A/R-TiO2 heterojunction and surface decoration of CQDs.

To further disclose the underlying charge transfer dynamics processes of the photoanodes, the intensity modulated photocurrent and photovoltage spectroscopy (IMPS/IMVS) techniques were performed. The IMPS/IMVS is a powerful tool to investigate the transport dynamics of photogenerated carriers. The electron collection time (τ c) and electron recombination time (τ r) are calculated by the Eqs. (9) and (10), respectively [46,47]:
(9)
τ c
=
1
2 π
f
m i n
( I M P S )
(10)
τ r
=
1
2 π
f
m i n
( I M V S )
where f min (IMPS) and f min (IMVS) are the frequency at the imaginary minimum in the IMPS and IMVS Nyquist plots, respectively. The electron collection time (τ c) represents the average time that the photogenerated electrons need to transfer from the film to the FTO conductive substrate and the electron recombination time (τ r) represents the lifetime of the majority carriers under open circuit conditions. The IMPS and IMVS Nyquist plots of TiO2, A/R-TiO2, CQDs/TiO2 and CQDs/A/R-TiO2 are presented in Fig. 8a and Fig. 8b, respectively. As shown in Fig. 8c , the τ c decreases in the order: TiO2 (20.9 ms) > CQDs/TiO2 (14.1 ms)> A/R-TiO2 (4.73 ms) > CQDs/A/R-TiO2 (1.84 ms), suggesting that the electrons in CQDs/A/R-TiO2 transfer more quickly than others. In addition, the τ r increases in the order: TiO2 (0.152 s) < A/R-TiO2 (0.731 s) < CQDs/TiO2 (2.98 s) < CQDs/A/R-TiO2 (10.6 s), suggesting the lowest electron recombination rate in CQDs/A/R-TiO2 (Fig. 8d). It is worth mentioning that the A/R-TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 show both improved τ c and τ r properties compared with TiO2, in which the A/R-TiO2 has better τ c but worse τ r properties than CQDs/TiO2.

To further investigate the competition between electron transfer and recombination in the photoanodes, the electron collection efficiency (η c) and electron average difussion length (L n) are calculated by the Eqs. (11) and (12), respectively [46,47]:
(11)
η c
= 1 -
τ c
τ r
(12)
L n
= d
τ r
2.35 τ
c
where d is the film thickness. As shown in Fig. 8d, the η c value of TiO2 is 86.2 %. However, the η c values of A/R-TiO2, CQDs/TiO2 and CQDs/A/R-TiO2 are remarkably increased to more than 99.4 %, suggesting the greatly increased collection of photogenerated electrons after formation of A/R-TiO2 heterojunction or decoration of CQDs. The L n values of TiO2, A/R-TiO2, CQDs/TiO2 and CQDs/A/R-TiO2 are all larger than their film thicknesses (∼3.0 μm), indicating that the electrons can transport across the photoanodes and be effectively collected by the external circuit. Compared with TiO2 (3.5 μm), the L n values of A/R-TiO2 (16.2 μm) and CQDs/TiO2 (19.0 μm) are significantly increased. With modification of CQDs on the surface of A/R-TiO2, the L n value is further increased to 98.9 μm, indicating a better performance of charge transfer dynamics.

The charge transfer kinetics processes of the photoanodes were also characterized by the transient photocurrent and photovoltage (TPC/TPV) techniques [48,49]. The TPC behaviors were monitored under short-circuit conditions, which determined the required time (τ c) that the charge transfer to the FTO conductive substrates from the films. The CQDs/A/R-TiO2 shows shorter τ c (2.07 ms) than TiO2 (5.64 ms), A/R-TiO2 (2.98 ms) and CQDs/TiO2 (3.64 ms) ( Fig. 9 a). The TPV behaviors were further traced under open circuit conditions, which were applied to extract information on carrier lifetime (τ v) within the phoanodes. As portrayed in Fig. 9b, the τ v of CQDs/A/R-TiO2 is 1.23 s, which is longer than that of TiO2 (0.63 s), A/R-TiO2 (0.72 s) and CQDs/TiO2 (0.74 s). The trends of the results are consistent with the IMPS/IMVS measurements, once again indicating the significantly increased carriers separation and transfer dynamics of CQDs/A/R-TiO2. The fitting results of TPC and TPV curves are shown in Table S3 and Table S4, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Energy band alignment and carriers transfer mechanism
ce:section.ce:para:
To better understand the transfer processes of photogenerated carriers, it is necessary to determine the energy band alignments of ternary CQDs/A/R-TiO2 heterojunction. The relative positions of valence band maximum (VBM) for both rutile TiO2 and anatase TiO2 were measured by the valence band (VB) XPS spectra. As shown in Fig. 10 a, the VBM of rutile TiO2 and anatase TiO2 are 1.81 eV and 2.76 eV, respectively. Therefore, the valence band offset (ΔE VBO) of A/R-TiO2 heterojunction is 0.95 eV [50,51]. According to the E g (3.00 eV for rutile TiO2 and 3.21 eV for anatase TiO2, respectively), the conduction band offset (ΔE CBO) of A/R-TiO2 heterojunction is 0.74 eV. The results suggest a direct Z-scheme band alignment for A/R-TiO2 heterostructure (Fig. S17a). In order to further confirm the Z-scheme structure of A/R-TiO2 heterojunction, the flat band potentials (V FB) were estimated by the Mott-Schottky plots. The V FB can be calculated according to the following Eq. [13]:
(13)
1
C 2
=
2
e ε
ε 0
N D
V -
V
F B
-
K B
T
e
where C is the space charge capacitance; e is the fundamental electron charge; ε is the relative permittivity of the semiconductor; ε0 is the permittivity of vacuum; N D is the carrier density; V is the applied potential; K B is the Boltzmann constant and T is the absolute temperature. Obtained by the X-axis intercept, the V FB of rutile TiO2 and anatase TiO2 are 0.0923 eV and 0.260 eV, respectively (Fig. 10b). For n-type semiconductor, the conduction band (CB) locates at the position that is 0.1–0.3 eV higher than the V FB position [13,52,53]. Assuming potential difference between the CB and V FB is 0.3 eV, the CB potentials of rutile TiO2 and anatase TiO2 are −0.208 eV and −0.04 eV vs. RHE, respectively. Thus, the VB potentials of rutile TiO2 and anatase TiO2 are 2.79 eV and 3.17 eV vs. RHE, respectively. The values of ΔE VBO and ΔE CBO are calculated to be 0.378 eV and 0.168 eV, respectively (Fig. S17b). The above methods determine a direct Z-scheme A/R-TiO2 heterostructure, in which the energy band alignment of rutile TiO2 and anatase TiO2 agrees well with the reported results [54–57]. The values of ΔE VBO and ΔE CBO obtained by the two methods are compared in Table S5. As shown in Fig. 10c, the Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO) or CB level of CQDs is estimated to be −0.31 V vs. RHE [58,59]. The absorption edge of CQDs locates at approximately 646.5 nm, giving the E g of 1.93 eV (Fig. S18). Therefore, the Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital (HOMO) or VB level of CQDs is 1.61 V vs. RHE. Based on the discussion above, the proposed energy band alignments and charge transfer mechanism of ternary CQDs/A/R-TiO2 heterojunction are illustrated in Fig. 10d.

Besides the role of serving as heterojunction, the CQDs can improve the visible light harvesting of A/R-TiO2 due to the upconversion effect originated from two or more photons absorption [11,60]. In short, the electrons in the π orbital can be excited and jump into the high-energy orbital (π* orbital) with the excitation of low-energy photons. Then the excited electrons drop back into the lower energy orbital (σ orbital) ascribed to the radiative relaxation, resulting in the emission of short-wavelength light [37]. The CQDs can enhance the ultraviolet light absorbing capacity and expand the range of light harvesting to the visible region, leading to fundamentally increased J abs. The photogenerated electrons in the LUMO level of CQDs are injected in the CB of anatase TiO2, which are easy to directly recombine with the photogenerated holes in the VB of rutile TiO2. Meanwhile, the photogenerated holes in the VB of anatase TiO2 are easy to transfer to the HOMO level of CQDs. Consequently, the photogenerated electrons in the CB of rutile TiO2 and the photogenerated holes in the VB of anatase TiO2 and CQDs are well spatially isolated, resulting in efficient hydrogen generation and water oxidation, respectively.

In summary, we have fabricated the CQDs/A/R-TiO2 photoanode with construction of A/R-TiO2 heterojunction and further surface modification of CQDs. The A/R-TiO2 heterojunction efficiently suppresses the bulk recombination of photogenerated carriers, while the CQDs simultaneously enhance the OER kinetics and UV–vis light harvesting, resulting in dramatically increased photocurrent and negatively shifted onset potential of CQDs/A/R-TiO2. This work may open an avenue for the rational design and bottom-up synthesis of efficient photoanodes for PEC applications.
Experimental section and additional figures as mentioned can be found in the Supporting Information.

Tingsheng Zhou: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Visualization, Formal analysis, Data curation, Validation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Shuai Chen: Investigation. Linsen Li: Formal analysis. Jiachen Wang: Formal analysis. Yan Zhang: Validation. Jinhua Li: Investigation, Data curation. Jing Bai: Supervision, Project administration, Resources, Funding acquisition. Ligang Xia: Visualization. Qunjie Xu: Visualization. Mohammadi Rahim: Validation. Baoxue Zhou: Conceptualization, Resources, Supervision, Project administration, Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition.
